coredata.dc:title: An insight into the solvatochromic and photophysical behaviours of biowaste-origin carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Mohammad-Jafarieh, Parisa
Salamat-Ahangari, Rahman
Pourhassan-Moghaddam, Mohammad
Akbarzadeh, Abolfazl
coredata.dc:description: In this work, C-dots were synthesised inexpensively, using hydrothermal, one-step pathway from persimmon peel biowaste. The prepared C-dots were investigated by UV–Vis spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy, XRD, TEM, CHN, FE-SEM and FT-IR. It was found that the C-dot is amorphous in nature, spherical in shape, narrow-sized, monodispersed, polar functionalised with average 2 nm size and highly dispersible in aqueous and non-aqueous media. Optical study in wide range of solvents, exhibited excitation wavelength dependency of photoluminescence, bathochromic and hypsochromic shifts in absorption and emission spectra and incredibly high quantum yields ranging from 10% to 60%. It was shown that the C-dot could be exploited as nanoprobe for polarity of medium in addition as fluorescent chemical sensing platform for some metal ions. Analyses resulted in different functional groups on the surface (carboxylic, carbonyl, amine, amide and hydroxyl) and led us to treat the solvatochromic method as convincing argument to resolve the dilemma of whether or not which functional groups are responsible for the intriguing observed optical properties. The increasing trend of emission efficiencies of homologues alcohols was interpreted in terms of exoergic (specific and non-specific) interaction parameters included in Kamlet-Taft’s equation along with the endoergic solubility parameters and correspondingly the stiffness of solvent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solvatochromism
Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence tunability
Absorption
Quantum yield
Biowaste

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support by the research vice president of Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University (Grant No. 601/97).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)TEM image, (b)particle size distribution histogram and (c) XRD patterns of C-dots.
FT-IR spectrum of C-dots indicate the presence of various functional groups on the surface of C-dots.
UV–vis absorption spectrum of C-dots in aqueous solution, [inset: photographs of C-dots under daylight (a) and UV light (b)]
Emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of C-dots dispersed in (a) water and in (b) 1,4-Dioxane.

Luminescence emission spectra of C-dots excited at 350 nm wavelength (3.54 eV) in various solvents. Maximum peak positions are indicated on each plot in eV. Spectra were vertically shifted for easier viewing and the data far from maximum positions were trimmed. Acronyms were defined in the experimental section.

(a) Emission and (b) absorption peaks of spectra of C-dots in different solvents as function of
E N T
solvent polarity parameters. Insets: respective emission (a) and absorption (b) of protic solvents illustrate steeper slope.

(a) Maximum PL intensity of C-dots in protic solvents as function of solvent relative permittivity. (b) Maximum absorption of C-dots in protic solvents as function of solvent electrostatic factor. (c) Quantum yield of C-dots dispersed in all the studied solvents as function of solvent relative permittivity. (d) Quantum yield as function of protic solvent
E N T
parameter. In all cases emissions were excited at 350 nm (3.54 eV photon energy) and for clarity, alcohols are shown in red.

Emission efficiency dependency of C-dots on the van der Waals molecular volume of the homologues alcohols with the inclusion of water, for comparison reason the QY results of C-dots from literature [42–44] under similar conditions are also included. Connecting lines are a guide to the eye.
Illustration of preparation of C-dots (CDs) from persimmon peel through hydrothermal method.
Relative contribution percentage of KAT parameters for fluorescence and absorbance of C-dots.

head.ce:title: An insight into the solvatochromic and photophysical behaviours of biowaste-origin carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, 35 km Tabriz-Maraghe Road, P. O. Box 53714161, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, 35 km Tabriz-Maraghe Road, P. O. Box 53714161, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medical Biotechnology, Faculty of Medical Sciences, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Medical Biotechnology, Faculty of Medical Sciences, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medical Nanotechnology, Faculty of Medical Sciences, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Medical Nanotechnology, Faculty of Medical Sciences, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, C-dots were synthesised inexpensively, using hydrothermal, one-step pathway from persimmon peel biowaste. The prepared C-dots were investigated by UV–Vis spectroscopy, photoluminescence spectroscopy, XRD, TEM, CHN, FE-SEM and FT-IR. It was found that the C-dot is amorphous in nature, spherical in shape, narrow-sized, monodispersed, polar functionalised with average 2 nm size and highly dispersible in aqueous and non-aqueous media. Optical study in wide range of solvents, exhibited excitation wavelength dependency of photoluminescence, bathochromic and hypsochromic shifts in absorption and emission spectra and incredibly high quantum yields ranging from 10% to 60%. It was shown that the C-dot could be exploited as nanoprobe for polarity of medium in addition as fluorescent chemical sensing platform for some metal ions. Analyses resulted in different functional groups on the surface (carboxylic, carbonyl, amine, amide and hydroxyl) and led us to treat the solvatochromic method as convincing argument to resolve the dilemma of whether or not which functional groups are responsible for the intriguing observed optical properties. The increasing trend of emission efficiencies of homologues alcohols was interpreted in terms of exoergic (specific and non-specific) interaction parameters included in Kamlet-Taft’s equation along with the endoergic solubility parameters and correspondingly the stiffness of solvent.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-dots) have emerged as new kind of zero-dimensional fluorescent nanomaterials with diameters less than 10 nm[1]. C-dots have attracted much attention because of their unique physical properties which have been exploited in biosensing[2,3], bio-imaging[4–6], drug delivery[7], catalysis[8,9], batteries[10] and other biomedical and biological applications[11–13]. The properties mentioned above include their excellent fluorescence, high quantum yield (QY), biocompatibility, low toxicity and good chemical stability[7,14,15].

C-dots are generally synthesised by top-down or bottom-up methods[16]. The main methods of the C-dots synthesis include laser ablation[17,18], microwave heating[19], hydrothermal treatment[20], electrochemical exfoliation[21] and ultrasonic[22]; from which the hydrothermal treatment is a cost-effective approach for synthesis of nanomaterials, especially C-dots and in stark contrast to other methods need not to inconvenient post-treatment steps. In other words, most of the aforementioned methods require expensive surface passivating agents to functionalise the carbon nanodots and enhancing luminescence efficiencies.

However, application of food biowastes as the carbon source adds value to the C-dots synthesis process in terms of cost and simplicity, in addition these renewable biomaterials are easily available. Other advantageous application of natural carbon sources is that the resultant C-dots have shown higher water-solubility, high photo stability, detection of pollutants and environmental sustainability[6,7,23].

Application of glucosamine[24], milk[25], honey[26], lemon juice[27], onion[28], tomato juice[29], orange juice[30], watermelon[31], sweet potato[32], banana[33], pear[34] and persimmon[35] as carbon bio-sources have been reported in the literature. Furthermore, in the past few years, a limited number of reports concerning food wastes as reproducible raw sources have been published on C-dots synthesis. For instances, Tyagi et al. reported a hydrothermal synthesis of C-dots of lemon peel biowaste, and in that, they have demonstrated its applicability for the detection of chromium (VI)[36]. Zhou et al. prepared a water-soluble C-dots by employing a carbonisation process of watermelon peel[37]. Dubey et al. used the hydrothermal technique and adopted soya-nugget as natural source[38], and an additional example of bio-waste extracted C-dots worth mentioning are pomelo peel[39], willow bark[40] and mingled large-scale food waste-derived sources[41].

These C-dots, commonly known as photoluminescence (PL) nano-carbons, are mainly composed of spherical graphitic carbon core and a functionalised shell. They possess high surface area to volume ratio and their PL originate in part from excitonic emission of surface energy traps or defects. Although the naturally synthesised C-dots exhibit several favourable properties. Still, their properties can be improved. Optical properties are the most tremendous aspect that can be improved in C-dots and require further examination.

It has been shown that solvents can affect the solute’s properties. In other words, the nature of the solvent can play an essential role in chemical structure and physical properties of the C-dots[42]. Most of the synthesised C-dots exhibit excellent solubility in wide varieties of organic and inorganic solvents[43]. The solvent-dependent properties, known as solvatochromism, have rarely been studied in the biomass/waste sourced C-dots.

Therefore, in this study persimmon (Diospyros kaki) peel, was used to develop a single-step and economic greener C-dots synthesis procedure based on hydrothermal synthesis without the need for expensive substances and elaborate experimental set-ups. Generally, in addition to carbohydrates the main constituents of persimmon peel are dietary fibre, high levels of antioxidants and vitamin C. Moreover, the quantity of bioactive compounds, mainly carotenoids and polyphenol is more significant in the peel compared to the pulp[44–46].

Correspondingly the crucial point of the present communication is the solvatochromism studies of the as-prepared C-dots dispersed in a broad range of solvents, the main parameters investigated included elucidation of general as well as specific solvent effect, solute–solvent interaction, and influence of physical and chemical environments on the nature, position, absorption and emission spectra.

Accordingly, fluorescence tunability is under the influence of both polar and apolar media, pH effect and quantum yield of C-dots were surveyed. An attempt was made to represent that the C-dot can be treated as nanoprobe of the polarity of the solvents and as chemical sensing platform of environmental heavy metals pollutant[7].

Fresh persimmon was purchased from local market (Tabriz, Iran) and the peels were collected in-house. Two different classes of solvents in these studies were used: protic (hydro bond) solvents and aprotic solvents. Although in the current study, usage of organic solvents was scarcely and restricted to some spectroscopic measurements, nonetheless, their safety, health and environmental criteria were considered according to CHEM 21-based solvent selection recommendations. [47] (ESI Table S1). The solution of metal ions employed in this study were prepared from their respective nitrate and chloride salts.

A known amount of persimmon peel was used as natural carbon rich precursor and a direct synthesis of C-dot was performed, exploiting one step hydrothermal treatment of the precursor at a mild temperature (120 °C) and through the dehydration a carbonaceous material was produced and in turn, leads to the formation of luminescence carbogenic nanoparticle. Interestingly, neither strong acid solvent nor surface passivation was required for PL to occur. The materials and synthesis procedure of C-dots (also denoted as CDs) are represented in detail in Electronic Supplementary Information and briefly illustrated in the simplified scheme 1 .

Selective detection of metal ions was performed at room temperature in phosphate buffer solution at pH 7. In each case 2 µL of C-dots (1 mg dispersed in 1 mL water) was added to 1 mL of 50 mM solution of the metal ions, and the PL emission spectra were recorded at 350 nm and all other experimental parameters were kept constant and each experiment was repeated for three times.

UV–Vis absorption spectra of C-dots were recorded by a CECIL CE7250 Spectrophotometer with a 1 cm quartz cuvette at room temperature. Meanwhile, photoluminescence (PL) measurements were undertaken using a Cytation 5/ Biotek/USA fluorescence spectrophotometer with excitation slit set at 1 nm pass and emission at 1 nm band pass in 96 cell plates. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was done by a TENSOR 27 FTIR spectrophotometer using a KBr pellets from 400 to 4000 cm−1. Besides elemental (CHN) analysis was performed using Perkin-Elmer 240B analyser.

The morphology, size and microstructure of C-dots were characterised using Transmission Electron Microscope, TEM/LEO 906/Zelss, (Germany) with an accelerating voltage of 100 kv, Field EmissionScanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM) and EDX, TESCAN/MIRA3, (CzechRepublic). For detecting dispensation and stokes diameter of as-prepared CD particles, Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and ζ-Potential of the CDs were registered using zetasizer Nano ZS/ZEN3600 (Malvern/England). Moreover, in order to sanalyse the crystalline structures and size, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were registered by using a BRUKER AXS\ D8 ADVANCE Diffractometer over the range of 2θ = (10–80) °.
The obtained C-dots display excellent solubility and strong photoluminescence both in water and organic solvents.

The morphology and particle size distribution of the C-dots were assessed by transmission electron microscope image (TEM) as shown in Fig. 1 a. The TEM image confirmed the formation of carbon dots derived from persimmon peel with an average size of 2 nm (Fig. 1b). The TEM results showed that C-dots are spherical in shape, narrow-sized, monodispersed and with spherical morphologies in the range of 1–7 nm that are well separated in aqueous media (see Fig. 1).

The XRD patterns of the C-dots, as shown in Fig. 1c, display a single broad peak around 20.1° again indicating a significant level of topological defect in the sample. The XRD results showed that the synthesised sample is amorphous in nature. The 002 peak about 2θ = 20.1° corresponds to a set of sp2 graphitic carbons. However, an increase in interlayer spacing (d) value of the C-dots (d = 0.44 nm) was detected compared to that of graphitic interlayer spacing (d = 0.33 nm). The increase in d value of carbon dots is due to the increased amorphous nature, which confirms the abundant presence of more oxygen-containing groups excited on the surface of C-dots4,5. In comparison to the preparation of C-dots from persimmon (the closest relative to persimmon peel) as demonstrated by a very recent study of Reddy et al. the result of the present investigation conforms to the amorphous nature of graphitic carbon and C3-N structure insight the core, unravelled by SAED, XPS and HRTEM methods[35].

In addition, the results of dynamic light scattering analysis for hydrodynamic diameter indicate a narrow size distribution (PDI less than 0.2) ranging around 10 nm for the synthesised C-dots in solvent (ESI Figure S1a) and without any precipitation after four months. Besides as the (ESI Figure S1b) illustrates, the zeta potential of the synthesised C-dots is calculated as –33.5 mV. The magnitude of this electrokinetic potential indicates the degree of electrostatic repulsion between charged particles (nanoparticles have a double layer of ions on their surface). In this respect –33.5 mV confers a high negative charge density on the surface, which leads to a high and long-term colloidal stability of the C-dots and this could be attributed to the carboxylic/carbonyl functional groups which is evidenced by FTIR as below.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) was employed to analyse the surface groups and the chemical structure of the as-prepared CDs as shown in Fig. 2 , a strong and broad peak of O-H and N-H stretching vibrations at 3378 cm−1 were observed and confirm the existence of oxygen and nitrogen-containing hydrophilic functional groups on the surface of C-dot. The peak at 2926 cm−1 was identified as the stretching and vibration of C-H and C = N, respectively (in case of as-prepared C-dot from persimmon pulp by Reddy et al. the presence of amide and amine functional groups were proved using XPS, Raman and NMR). The characteristic absorption band illustrated the existence of the stretching of C = O and C = C at 1625 cm−1. Similarly, the peak at 1448 cm−1 could be due to the presence of C-H, N-H, and –COO groups. Moreover, the peaks in the region of 778–918 cm−1 are probably assignable to aromatic C-H out of plane bending vibrations. The spectrum also reveals one small peak at 1346 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending of C-H. The small peak at 1252 cm−1 could corroborate the presence of the bending of C = O-C from ether or epoxy. Lastly the peak at 1057 cm−1 probably originated from the stretching vibration of C-O. Based on FTIR spectra analysis results, the synthesised carbon dot contains functional groups including –COOH, and –OH, which results in the excellent solubility of C-dots without the use of any chemical modifications.

Additionally, elemental analysis (CHN analyser) of the dried sample demonstrated a carbon content of 46.1 ± 0.4%, hydrogen 7.88 ± 0.2% and nitrogen content of 10.02 ± 0.7%. Likewise, morphological details of the C-dots were obtained using Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM); as shown in ESI Figure S2 a-e, FE-SEM revealed the presence of well-dispersed and roughly spherical shape and in line with symmetrical distributions of C-dots. Further examination of the chemical structure of C-dots using EDX confirmed the presence of C and O in the C-dots samples (ESI Figure S2 f). The most abundant element in the obtained C-dot is carbon, and oxygen is the second-most abundant element in C-dots. Although the EDX analysis of light elements (C, O, H, N, …) are poorly quantitative, the ratios and ample values of oxygen (ca. 36% calculated) approve why the carbogenic was coined as a convenient term for these C-dots, and obviously as result of dehydration the carbon fraction increases at the expense of the loss of hydrogen and oxygen.

UV–Vis spectra: the UV–Vis spectrum of C-dots is shown in Fig. 3 , the C-dots have an optical absorption in UV region, with an end extended to the visible region. The absorption edge at 255 nm (4.8 eV) induces the π-π* transitions domains of sp2 hybridised of conjugated carbon rings embedded in the sp3 matrix. This part of absorption spectrum by proceeding beyond the approximately 4.8 eV has a steep slope and generally is regarded as bandgap transition of the graphitic core of C-dots.

According to the framework elaborated by Reckmeier et al.[48] the core is terminated at the edge in contact with the heteroatom functional group of the peripheral surface (see graphical abstract) and the absorption peak located at 277 nm is assigned to the n-π* and is due to the transition of C = O (together with π-π* intramolecular-like charge transfers) at this edge band and its surface. This is the characterisation of the C-dots prepared by the carbonisation of carbon-based materials. As demonstrated in the inset of Fig. 3, the as-prepared C-dot solution exhibited a brownish-yellow visible colour in the visible region and exhibited a green–blue fluorescence in the UV region.

Emission spectra: excitation-dependent PL of the aqueous dispersion of studied C-dots is depicted in Fig. 4 a. The resulted multicolour luminescence is mainly associated with the transitions at the edge of the C-dot core and pursuing in part the above approach; this can be treated as the emissive centres.

The maximum emission peak with the greatest intensity of the prepared C-dot in water is 450 nm (2.75 eV) when it is excited at 350 nm (3.54 eV) this value might be construed as 2.75 eV of mid-gap energy in the edge states. In conformity with the general observation from various researchers, in the present study, C-dots demonstrated a progressively red shift of emission spectra with the increase in excitation wavelengths and the Fig. 4a reveals that the least intensity is located at 542 nm upon excitation at 410 nm (2.28 eV excited at 3.02 eV), this
λ
ex
-dependency is a familiar finding by other investigators and is the hallmark of photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles.

Noticeably, wavelength excitation below 2.28 eV energies fails to induce any measurable fluorescence (Fig. 4). However, the mechanism for this tunable fluorescence is still a matter of current debate and requires good clarification. In accordance with the literature and the framework mentioned above, a vital clue to understanding this behaviour is the phenomenon that has been found in the intimate correspondence of semiconductor nanoparticles and this is a growing consensus to date[6,18,49,50].

Taken together, mechanism of this photoluminescence is mainly attributable to the differently sized C-dots and or surface-related defective sites distributed on the surface and lead to different energy traps. In other words, an exciton can be created when the C-dot absorbs a photon of higher energy than its bandgap (in this work exciting energies higher than 2.28 eV as an implication of sub-bandgap of edge states). In this line a colourful luminescence observed due to radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs of excitons, besides non-radiative recombination may occur through energy release via phonon emissions or other quenching mechanisms.

Excitation-dependent PL of the as-prepared C-dots dispersed in the least polar aprotic dioxane and 1-propanol solvents are illustrated in Fig. 4b and ESI Fig. S3 respectively, and the same manner of tunability elucidated above is in a lesser extent correct in this respect. As can be seen in ESI Fig. S3, although the photoluminescence behaviour of C-dot in 1-propanol shows lesser dependency on the excitation wavelengths, the extents of PL intensities are superior compared to the more polar solvents under study and for alcohols will be more intense in case of butanol. On the contrary, the PL intensities of C-dots in the dioxane (consider as apolar) are somehow quenched, similar results are observed for acetonitrile and DMF.

Except for those systems require surface passivation for PL to occur, research on excitation-independency behaviour of C-dots, such as Yu and colleagues were scarce[51]. Likely the optical behaviours of C-dots in various solvent studied by Mukherjee et al. the C-dot did not exhibit any excitation-dependency in aprotic solvents, and they have assigned their observation merely to H-bonding interactions on the surface of C-dot[52].

The photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) of the aqueous dispersion of C-dots was calculated to be 23.5% (by use of quinine sulfate as detailed in the Electronic Supplementary Information) this value is appreciably greater than the QY of other C-dots in water solvent, and increases as the lipophilic character of the solvent promotes. The desired value of QY are ascribed to smaller C-dots particle size and stems from larger surface-to-volume ratio[6,30]. The quantum yield of synthesised C-dots was studied in the range of protic and aprotic solvents and the results are reported in (ESI Table S2).

pH dependency and sensing platform of metal ions: variation of fluorescence intensity with the pH of the solution is demonstrated in (ESI Fig. S4), and as can be seen the maximum PL intensity of aqueous dispersion of C-dot are intriguingly high within the interval of 6–8, whereas for higher or lower pH values the PL intensity decreases. One possible explanation for this significant decrease of PL intensity as the acidity increases could be due to the protonation of nitrogen-containing functional groups; as a result, this protonation lowers the electron-donating efficiency[53]. In spite of that, a milder decrease of PL intensity from pH 8 to 14 could be accounted for the deprotonation of the carboxyl group on the surface of C-dot[6,7,18,48].

We explored the feasibility of utilising as-prepared C-dot for chemical sensing and detection of some metal ions. It is observed that C-dots solution in the presence of Fe3+ and Pb2+ (among all metal ions as shown in ESI Fig. S5) exhibits a dramatic decrease in PL intensity and this detection is more sensitive to lead ion. Similar observations were found in some researches on C-dots, and as reiterated by Giannelis and co-workers, this quenching effect was attributed to the formation of a stable non-fluorescence complex between C-dots and metal ions[53]. Accordingly, in the present work, the intense quenching provoked by heavy metal Pb2+ presumably arises from stronger metal-fluorophore complexation between C-dots and lead ions[36,39]. In the sense of iron(III), one mechanism that cannot be ruled out is the quenching of C-dots by Fe3+, inherited from half-filled 5d orbital of iron cation, given that, the electrons at the conduction band of C-dots can be transferred there quickly[27,36,53].

Solvatochromic study: C-dots’ optical spectroscopic behaviours are solvent-sensitive and can be employed as solvatochromic indicators, meanwhile in order to study mutual interaction of CD-solvent, we dispersed the C-dots in an extensive range of protic and aprotic solvents with dissimilar solvation capabilities (ESI Table S2), the resulting emission spectra showed a robust solvent-dependent shift as shown in Fig. 5 , the results from fluorescence spectra revealed a bathochromic (or red) shift with increasing solvent polarity. We found the fluorescence red-shifts with enhancing solvent polarity from 3.36 eV maximum of peak position in dioxane (the least polar with dielectric constant 2.3), to the studied blue most fluorescence 2.75 eV in water (the most polar with ε = 78.5), representing a maximum shift of 0.61 eV. This solvent-dependent shift value considering our solvent selection is higher than other solvatochromic studies we observed in other investigations on the optical properties of C-dots[43,50,52,54], and this phenomenon is ordinarily associated with the stabilisation of the Frank-Condon excited state of C-dots by increasing the polarity of solvents.

Conversely, the results of UV/vis/near-IR spectra exhibited colouful absorption bands and the peak values were displayed in Fig. S6. As seen in figure, these results imply that with increasing solvent polarity, a hypsochromic (or blue) shift is observed in the absorption spectra (ESI Fig. S6) where the maximum shift (discrepancy between the least polar and the most polar medium) was 0.58 eV. Accordingly, it can be inferred that the ground-state of C-dots is predominately affected by solvation.

The maximum electronic transition energy values, ET, of the C-dots in a wide variety of solvents were evaluated employing Eq. (1)
(1)
E T
kJ . m o
l
- 1
=
h
c
N A
/
λ max
nm
=
119626 /
λ max
nm
where the ET known as solvent polarity scale and as a general solvation capability, demonstrates the specific (HB) and nonspecific interactions between the solute and solvent molecules. The C-dots solvatochromism can be effectively described by this quantity, for this reason, Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b illustrate a plot of emission and absorption peak positions versus
E N T
(normalised value of ET taken from tabulated values of literature) respectively. In the insets of Fig. 6, the trend for protic solvents was appended. An inspection of Fig. 6 reveals that in both cases the peak positions are approximately a linear function of the solvent polarity, and for emission, the linearity is of higher quality (
R 2
= 0.94
) in addition the linearities and steep slope for protic solvents are more significant and should be taken into account. The same observations were demonstrated in literature [42,46] under similar circumstances.

To further substantiate the medium effect on C-dots the Kamlet-Taft (KAT) equation was exploited, wherein the solvents’ effect defined by a linear solvation-energy relationship (LSER) expressed as[42,55].
(2)
XYZ =
XYZ
0
+
a α
+
b β +
s
π ∗
Where XYZ in the present study stands for ET and the parameter α, β and
π ∗
in Eq. (2) are a quantitative, empirical measure of the ability of a hydrogen bond donor (HBD), hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA) and dipolarity/polarizability of solvent, respectively. The regression coefficients a, b and s measure the relative susceptibilities of the solvent dependence of ET scale. The solvatochromism parameters (α, π*, and β) of Kamlet–Taft’s scales, along with
E N T
and other solvent properties, namely, dielectric constants, the electron-accepting number (AN), dipole moment (µ) and viscosity are tabulated in (ESI Table S2).

The maximum wavelengths of the absorption and emission spectra of as-synthesised C-dots (as quoted above and indicated in Fig. 5 and ESI Fig. S6) were converted to ET by use of Eq.1 and the obtained ET were correlated with solvent properties[56] employing Multiple Linear Regression (MLR) analysis using equation (2). Consequently, optimisation of Kamlet–Taft’s equation yielded the values of XYZ0, a, b, s along with statistical parameters consisting; the R-squares coefficient, and the overall standard error (ose) of ET (in brackets) and are listed in (ESI Table S3). As shown in the Table, excellent results were achieved when all three parameters were included in the fitting, in spite of that an inspection of the optimisation data reveals that by ignoring basicity and dipolarity/polarizability parameters the results are not significantly affected, whereas, by setting acidity coefficient equal to zero the fit considerably deteriorates. Subsequently, the relative percentage impact of α, β and π ∗ were calculated and are represented in Table 1 .
The most significant results of the correlation of KAT equations can be described as below:

Emission spectra: the optimisation results by considering all coefficients of α, β and π ∗ in the correlation analysis of the multi-parameter KAT equation with emission data were found to be the best fit with R2 = 0.95 coefficient. The negative sign of the coefficients a and s dictating the α and π ∗ parameters in fluorescence, give rise to bathochromic shifts and signifies that the emitter centers in C-dots are sensitive to HBD character of solvents and polarisation of the excited state. In contrast, the effect of basicity (positive coefficient of β) is in the reverse direction. The greater relative contribution of α parameter (as shown in the third row of Table 1) demonstrates that the capability of the indicator C-dots to form hydrogen bonds with HBD solvent, plays a major role in quantifying PL values in different solvents. Notably the negative sign of its coefficient, states that the solvent acidity has lion’s share of inducing an increase in the stability of the first excited state rather than the ground state. Correspondingly as illustrated in (ESI Fig. S4) the solvatochromic findings observed above (and the decreasing trend of QY as explained below) is entirely consistent with the decreasing trend of PL intensities by lowering the pH of the medium.

Absorption spectra: again by including all three parameters in optimisation, better correlation was achieved and the positive sign of the coefficients of α and π ∗ in absorbance indicates that the C-dots probe encountering HBD solvents and non-specific dielectric interaction stabilise the ground state rather than the excited state and as mentioned above the resultant effect is the occurrence of an hypsochromic shifts (Fig. 6b). Nonetheless, β parameter has the reverse effect and the relative contribution percentage of parameters for absorption (the greater value for coefficients α in the second row of Table 1), hints the impact of α value is less pronounced compared to that achieved in the correlation of KAT for emission energies. Hence in absorption spectra, the influence of HB basicity and polarisation parameter could be slightly more considerable compared to emission spectra.

To put it concisely, for fluorescence and absorption solvatochromism parameters, i.e., α, β and π* relative contribution are of the order of (Pα˃ Pβ˃ P π*); this result implies that the acidity parameter plays a foremost role. In spite that, the detail of correlation results in Table ESI S2 reveals that by excluding β and π* the results remained satisfactory. In other words, upon setting β equals zero, good correlation with small standard error does exist and is indicative of the fact that the solvent Lewis basicity should not be important to the C-dots/solvent interactions and this parameter might be disregarded. To scrutinise the issue, this could be a confirmation of HBA and non-electron pair acceptor (non-EPA) traits of functional groups on the surface of C-dot under consideration and consequently imply that functional group comprised of carbonyl groups are of minor contribution to the optical properties of C-dots.

Strictly speaking, oxygen- and nitrogen-containing surface groups can act as an electron donor (EPD) and produce an overall negative (–33.5 mV) zeta-potential further corroborate the profound involvement of surface moieties in the optical demonstration and their mechanism.

Once, as delineated in the Fig. 6a and b (and in the insets) the latter effect is enormously considerable for the protic solvents, hence we have shown their behaviours separately in Fig. 7 (a-d), in that the eclectic alcohols are shown in red and are among the preferred list of solvents by Pfizer green chemistry group[57].

Furthermore, an examination of Fig. 5 and Fig. 6a shows that the intervals of fluorescence peak positions are closer to each other in the case of aprotic solvents and the discrepancy among protic solvents (Fig. 6a and Fig. 7a) are remarkable and as illustrated in the figures (Fig. 5 and Fig. 6a) the distinctness between protic solvents and aprotic solvents is significant. Consequentially, as an explanation for the sensitivity of luminescence response to the various media, mechanistically, the HBA character of C-dot is more promoted by photoexcitation, probably due to an increase of the negative charge localised amongst the HBA groups on the surface of C-dots, a matter of fact that approved by other spectroscopic methods, as in literature [42,44] and the reference therein.

Similarly, if we consider the absorption peak of C-dot dispersed in various solvents, again these maximum peak position values increase systematically and are more distant from each other for protic solvents, but in the case of aprotic solvents we did not recognise a clear trend (Fig. 6b and ESI Fig. S6). The sensitivity of absorption to solvent polarity, especially confronting HB solvents may be attributable to
n -
π ∗
transition involves charges from the outer part of edge atom excite into
π ∗
orbital of the core, and this movement of charges induce a desirable change of dipole moment and favours the absorption. To demonstrate this remarkable increasing trend of absorption peaks for protic solvents the absorption peak positions of C-dot in Fig. 7b, were plotted against electrostatic factor EF (
EF = ε
μ
,
ε
: dielectric constant;
μ
: dipole moment of solvent).

Not surprisingly, to develop a correlation between solvent properties with absorption and PL behaviour, by plotting these peak positions and emission efficiencies against other exoergic properties of protic solvents; such as solvent electron-accepting number (AN for EPA Solvent), solvent electron-donating number (EPD solvent) and solute/solvent endoergic Hildebrand solubility parameter
δ H
, solvent stiffness and physical properties of solvent such as van der Waals molecular volume and viscosity, the resulted trends observed were in a fashion analogous to the Fig. 7 (a-d).

Correspondingly the variabilities of quantum yield (QY) of the synthesised C-dot dispersed in all used solvents (350 nm excitation) are illustrated in Fig. 7c. As expected a meaningful reduction for protic solvent are represented as the solvent polarity increased, the dependency of QY for carbon nanodot on relative permittivity of solvent was observed in a majority of investigations [42,44,49,52]. Ordinarily, attempts are made to find an exponential interrelation. Noticeably, relaxation of energy through non-radiative pathways are affected by solvent polarity as highlighted by Messina and co-workers the non-radiative rates exhibited an increasing trend with ET in protic solvents and causes a substantial reduction of the QY (Fig. 7d). One possible explanation of this photoinduced electron transfer as studied by Y.-P. Sun and colleagues[58], the C-dots may act as electron donators, and their PL emission is quenched by the solvents that prone to accept these electrons, in that the authors have shown that the solvent with greater AN (higher Stern-Volmer quenching constants) were more effective quencher, unquestionably this is the case consistent with the pictures delineated in Fig. 7c and 7d.

Accordingly, the high quantum yield of the C-dots dispersion in diethyl ether could be due in part to the minimum value of AN among the other aprotic solvents under investigation. In this context, it is tacitly assumed that, although a combination of various factors such as carbon-core domain are affecting on emissions, nonetheless, solvatochromism reveals the dominant influence of surface groups and regarding the organic solvents the specific effects such as hydrogen bonding were minimal. Hence, if we restrict our attention to the electron donating and accepting characters of the constituents, the functional groups on the surface of C-dots (treating as if they are discrete molecular fluorophore) containing – NH2 and –OH groups, act as electron donors in their excited states; thus the surface energy traps are stabilised in the vicinity of less polar solvents with lower AN value and eventually enhanced emission efficiency results[52].

Lastly, because of the complexity of surface groups and diversity of C-dots with their colloidal solution-like entity the mechanism of medium effect on C-dots remains an open question, throughout the published literature concerning solvation studies on C-dots, far too little attention has been paid to their thermodynamic endoergic solute–solvent interactions. On that account, within the framework of Kamlet-Taft equation, besides the exoergic interactions (α, π*, and β) , another term
m
δ H 2
will be added to equation (2), given that
δ H 2
is the squared of Hildebrand solubility parameter
δ
H
. This parameter represents the endoergic contribution to solute–solvent interaction, also known as cohesive energy density and indicates the work of making room for the solute, that is, of creating a hard cavity in the solvent to accommodate the solute. It was found that this quantity directly depends on the association of solvent molecules and is related to the solvent tightness and to the solvent free volume[59,60]. It has also been claimed that[61], HBD solvents upon association in the liquid forms, possess large cohesive energy densities and for alcohols under study; methanol, ethanol, propanol and butanol, the Hildebrand parameters at 298 K are respectively, 14.4, 12.92, 11.9 and 11.3
ca
l
1 / 2
.
c
m
- 3 / 2
and (the squared values as well) are inversely proportional to their molecular volumes[60]. The property of solvent stiffness (
δ H 2
-
p int
;
p int
is the internal pressure of solvent) which is a measure of the solvent structuredness and its decreasing trend is quite similar to the Hildebrand parameters.

Correspondingly, in the present study, the dependency of the QY of C-dots in homologues alcohols on the van der Waals molecular volume of methanol through butanol has a meaningful trend and should be taken into consideration. A survey of literature on the emission efficiency of C-dots in different alcohols[50–52] has shown that this dependency is not incidental; hence we made an effort to illustrate them in one figure. As shown in Fig. 8 , in all cases the quantum yields of C-dots increases systematically by going from low molecular weight alcohol to the higher alcohols (in accord with increasing trend of van der Waals volumes).

Accordingly, taking into consideration the fourth parameter in equation (2) after optimisation for emission efficiencies (QY) values of the studied C-dots in alcohols, despite the poor correlation, the negative values of
δ H 2
coefficient (m = -0.024), revealing that by decreasing cohesive energy densities ongoing from methanol to butanol, the increasing trend of QY was observed. This poor quality of correlation (R2 = 0.51) has been discussed before[61] and was attributed to the inconsistency among exoergic and endoergic interactions parameters and it was commonly observed for protonic solvent and termed as semi-quantitative approach[62]. However, the variation of quantum yield, could be explained on the basis of cohesive energy density and stiffness of the solvents, by growing the aliphatic part of the alcohols, i.e. the less tightness resulted in less effective collisions between higher alcohols and C-dots, the phenomena that is in complete accordance with the dependency of QY of organic dyes on the viscosity of solvents, namely, the higher the viscosity of solvent accompanying with the lower dissipation of radiational energy by external conversion rates.

In summary, we present a facile, cost-effective green hydrothermal method to prepare a luminescent carbogenic nanodot, with various polar functional groups on the surface. An inexpensive and readily accessible natural precursor was exploited, and no further chemical pre- and post-treatment was needed to prepare the C-dots. The prepared C-dots exhibited a remarkable excitation wavelength-dependent and pH-dependent emission in UV–Vis range, good solubility and high quantum yield in a broad span of aqueous and organic solvents. The synthesised C-dots illustrated the promising potential for the detection of some heavy metal ions and as nanoprobe for the sensitivity to solvent medium. Additionally, solvatochromism provided a semi-quantitative method to analyse the C-dot behaviours in connection with its surface-localised electronic transitions in terms of non-specific and specific solute–solvent mutual interactions. The systematic increasing of emission efficiencies of C-dots in homologues alcohols were correlated with solvent structuredness, and to our knowledge, it was unexplored to date.

With the development of new materials and methods by shifting the synthesis of carbon dots from chemicals such as graphene, etc. to biological and natural sources inerasably expanded, in this research, persimmon skin was used as a carbon source, and accompanied with interesting photo-physical and solvatochromic behaviours. This type of synthetic carbon dot now has promising applications in medicine, environment and energy. Our next step in studies related to this carbon dot will be in medical and biological applications such as cancer cell detection, disease treatment and the use of less toxic nanoparticles as drug delivery vehicles. Fundamentally, at least a fluorescence lifetime study and a computational method is required to produce more evidence for versatile functional groups and clarifying solvatochrimism mechanism, possible aggregation and their steric hindrance effect. In addition, modulation of photophysical properties and solubility of carbogenic quantum dots (C-dots) in various solvent is necessary for solvent engineering and optoelectronic applications. Eventually, investigation into the intriguing behaviour of C-dot in mixed aqueous-organic solvent, differential solvation considering the ground and excited state dipole moments and preferential solvation is underway and will be presented in our ongoing publication.

Parisa Mohammad-Jafarieh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Software, Visualization. Rahman Salamat-Ahangari: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Mohammad Pourhassan-Moghaddam: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Resources, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Abolfazl Akbarzadeh: Supervision, Project administration.
